comment: Lesson content by stage. Step types: text {heading?, body}, tip {body}, chord {chordId, body?}, practice {heading?, body?, checklist[]}, metronome {bpm, body}, trainer {pair:[a,b], bpm, body} (embedded chord-change trainer), rhythm {pattern, chordId?, bpm?, body} (embedded backing rhythm), strum {heading?, body?, pattern:[D|U|-], counts?} (strum notation). Stages 1-4 in Phase 1-2, stage 5 in Phase 3.

stages.title: First Steps
stages.subtitle: Get comfortable and make your very first sounds.

lessons.title: Meet your guitar
lessons.summary: Get comfy and learn the six strings.

steps.heading: Get comfy
steps.body: Sit up in a chair with both feet on the floor. Rest the curve of the guitar on your right leg and let it hug your body. There's no wrong way to feel relaxed.

steps.heading: The six strings
steps.body: Your guitar has six strings. The thickest one is at the top and the thinnest at the bottom. We count them from thickest (6th) to thinnest (1st).
Drop your shoulders and unclench your jaw. Loose and relaxed always sounds better than tense.

steps.heading: Try it
steps.body: Spend a minute just holding your guitar and getting comfortable.
checklist: Rest the guitar on your leg so it doesn't slide
Let your left hand gently cradle the neck
Keep your strumming arm loose and relaxed

lessons.title: Your first sounds
lessons.summary: Strum across the strings and pluck them one by one.

steps.heading: Give it a strum
steps.body: Brush your thumb (or a pick) gently down across all the strings, from thickest to thinnest. Hear that? You're playing guitar already.

steps.heading: One string at a time
steps.body: Now pluck each string on its own, slowly, from the thickest to the thinnest. Listen to how each one sounds a little higher than the last.
Aim for a smooth, even brush when you strum — not too hard. You're stroking the strings, not hitting them.
Get used to the feel of the strings.
checklist: Strum down slowly five times
Pluck each string one by one
Notice which strings sound low and which sound high

lessons.title: Your first chord: E minor
lessons.summary: Learn Em — the friendliest first chord.

steps.heading: What's a chord?
steps.body: A chord is a few notes played together to make one full sound. E minor (Em) is the perfect first one — it uses just two fingers.
Place your fingers on the two dots shown. Both go on the second fret, on the two strings next to the thickest one.
Press just behind the metal fret line, not on top of it, and press firmly. Curl your fingers so they don't touch the other strings.

steps.heading: Strum it
steps.body: With your fingers in place, strum all six strings slowly. It should ring out clearly.
checklist: Both fingers pressing on the second fret
Strum all six strings
Every string rings clearly — no buzzing

steps.body: Now let's add a steady beat. Start the metronome and strum Em once on every click.

stages.title: Making Music
stages.subtitle: Add a second chord and change between them in time.

steps.heading: A new shape
steps.body: A minor (Am) uses three fingers, but it sits right next to the Em shape you already know. You've got this.
Place your three fingers on the dots shown. Don't strum the thickest string — start your strum from the second-thickest.
Keep your fingertips curled and press with the very tips, so each string can ring out freely.
Get the Am shape feeling natural.
checklist: All three fingers in place
Strum from the 5th string down (skip the thickest)
Every note rings clearly

lessons.title: Your first chord change
lessons.summary: Switch smoothly between Em and Am.

steps.heading: Switching chords
steps.body: The magic of guitar is changing between chords. Em and Am are close cousins, so this is a great first switch to learn.
Start on Em.
Then move to Am. Notice your fingers only shift a little.
Go slowly. It's completely normal for changes to feel clumsy at first — every guitarist started exactly here.
Set a slow beat. Strum Em for four clicks, then Am for four clicks, and keep looping.
Loop the change until it feels smoother.
checklist: Four strums of Em, four strums of Am
Keep the beat steady — don't rush the change
Let it sound a little messy at first; it gets easier fast

lessons.title: Strum in time
lessons.summary: Play steady downstrokes with the beat.

steps.heading: Downstrokes
steps.body: A strumming pattern is just a rhythm. We'll start with the simplest: one downward strum on every beat.
Start the beat and strum Em down once per click. Aim to land right on each click.
Count out loud: 1, 2, 3, 4. Counting really does make your timing steadier.

steps.heading: Put it together
steps.body: Combine everything you've learned into a tiny piece of music.
checklist: Strum Em in time for four beats
Change to Am and strum four beats
Loop it — that's a real progression you're playing!

stages.title: Finding Your Rhythm
stages.subtitle: Strum real patterns and change chords in time.

lessons.title: Changing chords smoothly
lessons.summary: Switch between chords without stopping the beat.

steps.heading: Keep the beat moving
steps.body: The secret to smooth chord changes isn’t fast fingers — it’s not stopping. Keep your strumming hand going and switch a hair early, just before the next beat lands.
Move all your fingers as one shape, like a claw that lifts and lands together. Placing them one at a time is what makes changes feel slow.
Start the trainer and switch to the highlighted chord each time the box lights up. Slow and steady wins here.

steps.heading: Try it
steps.body: Loop the change until it starts to feel smoother.
checklist: Switch on every bar with the trainer
Keep your strumming hand moving through the change
Let it sound messy at first — it always smooths out

lessons.title: Reading strumming
lessons.summary: Learn down, up and misses — then strum along.

steps.heading: A simple map
steps.body: Strumming patterns use just three marks: D means strum down, U means strum up, and a dash (–) means miss the strings on that beat.

steps.heading: All downstrokes
steps.body: The simplest pattern of all: one downward strum on every beat.
Press play and strum Em down on every beat along with the app.
Let your strumming arm swing from the elbow like a pendulum — loose and even, never stiff.
Get comfortable with steady downstrokes.
checklist: Strum down on 1, 2, 3 and 4
Stay relaxed and even
Land right on each beat

lessons.title: The D-DU-UDU pattern
lessons.summary: Learn the most-used strum in pop and folk.

steps.heading: The famous one
steps.body: This pattern is in a huge number of songs. Count it as 1-and-2-and-3-and-4-and: your hand strums down on the numbers and up on the ands.

steps.heading: D · DU · UDU
steps.body: Keep your hand moving the whole time. On a miss, your hand still swings — it just doesn’t touch the strings.
Don’t stop your hand on the misses. That steady up-and-down motion is what keeps the rhythm locked in.
Play it slowly with the app on a G chord until your hand finds the groove.
Practise the pattern slowly.
checklist: Count 1-and-2-and out loud
Keep your hand moving on the misses
Speed up only once it feels natural

lessons.title: Counting and 4/4
lessons.summary: Understand the beat most songs are built on.

steps.heading: What 4/4 means
steps.body: Most songs are in 4/4: the beats come in groups of four. You count 1, 2, 3, 4 over and over, and each group of four is called a bar.
Start the beat and count out loud with it: 1, 2, 3, 4. Tap a little harder on the 1.
The 1 is the strongest beat of each bar. Leaning on it is what makes your playing feel steady and musical.
Feel the groups of four.
checklist: Count 1-2-3-4 with the metronome
Emphasise the 1 each time
Notice how the count loops around

lessons.title: Pattern and changes together
lessons.summary: Combine a strum pattern with a chord change.

steps.heading: The real skill
steps.body: Playing songs means doing two things at once: strumming a pattern and changing chords. We’ll build up to it gently.
First, practise the G to C change with the trainer until it feels comfortable.
When you add strumming, don’t freeze your hand to change chords. A missed string during a change is completely fine — keep the rhythm going.

steps.heading: Bring it together
steps.body: Now try strumming while you change.
checklist: Strum downstrokes, four per bar
Change G to C on each new bar
Keep strumming even if a change is a little late

steps.heading: Play a real song
steps.body: Head to the Songs tab and open a starter song in the studio. Choose App Rhythm and play along slowly — you’re making real music now!

stages.title: New Chords & Big Wins
stages.subtitle: Add bright new chords — and face the famous F.

lessons.title: E and A major
lessons.summary: Two bright majors used in loads of songs.

steps.heading: Two big majors
steps.body: E major and A major are bright, happy-sounding chords. E is the same shape as Em with one extra finger, so you already have a head start.
Place your fingers as shown and strum all six strings. Hear how bright it sounds next to Em.
Three fingers sit in a little row on the second fret. Start your strum from the second-thickest string.
A can feel crowded. Press with arched fingertips so every string still rings out.
Practise switching between E and A with the trainer.
Get both shapes ringing cleanly.
checklist: Every string rings on E
Fingers arched so A rings clearly
Switch between them slowly

steps.heading: A gentle sound
steps.body: D minor (Dm) has a soft, slightly sad sound. It’s a small shape on the thinner strings.
Place the three fingers as shown. Strum from the D string down — leave the two thickest strings out.
Only strum the thinnest four strings. Hitting the low strings will muddy the chord.
Play along and listen to that soft Dm sound ring out.
Make Dm feel familiar.
checklist: Three fingers in place
Strum only the thinnest four strings
Every note rings clearly

lessons.title: Putting E, A and Dm together
lessons.summary: Move between your new chords in time.

steps.heading: Mix them up
steps.body: Now let’s connect your new chords. Moving between A and Dm is a common, great-sounding change to practise.
Switch between A and Dm with the trainer. Notice how close the shapes are.
Look for a finger that can stay put or barely move between chords — that’s your anchor.
Loop the changes.
checklist: Switch A to Dm on each bar
Keep your hand relaxed
Try E to A to Dm in a row once it feels good

lessons.title: The F chord (the honest truth)
lessons.summary: Learn an easy F now — the barre can wait.

steps.heading: Let’s be honest
steps.body: F is the chord every beginner dreads. Here’s the truth: the full barre F takes everyone weeks, sometimes months. Struggling with it is completely normal and says nothing about your talent.

steps.heading: Start with easy F
steps.body: So we start with a friendlier version. This mini F uses only four strings, sounds great, and gets you playing F chords today.
Press the four thinnest strings as shown and strum just those four. That’s a real, usable F.
Lay your index finger flat across the two thinnest strings at the first fret, and strum only the four strings shown.
Play along with your easy F. Sounds good, doesn’t it?

steps.heading: Optional challenge: the full barre F
steps.body: When you’re ready — no rush at all — here’s the full barre F. Lay your index finger flat across all six strings at the first fret. Expect buzzing and muted strings for a while. That’s true for absolutely everyone.
The full barre F. Treat this as a long-term goal, not today’s homework. A little practice each day is all it takes.

steps.heading: You did it
steps.body: You’ve reached the F chord — a milestone loads of players never get to. Be proud.
checklist: Play the easy F cleanly on four strings
Try one slow barre F, just to feel it
Come back to the barre a little each day
